content.content:
1→# Habitat Skills
     2→
     3→This is a collection of AI agent skills for founders. Skills are markdown files in `skills/` that provide structured frameworks for building startups.
     4→
     5→## Key conventions
     6→
     7→- Every skill has a `SKILL.md` with YAML frontmatter (name, description)
     8→- Skills should check for `.claude/founder-context.md` before gathering context
     9→- Keep skill files under 500 lines
    10→- Reference related skills to help with routing
    11→- All skills follow the same pattern: gather context → apply framework → deliver actionable output
    12→
    13→## First-time users
    14→
    15→When a user starts a new session and no `.claude/founder-context.md` exists in their project, suggest running the onboarding skill: "Looks like this is your first time. Say **get started** and I'll walk you through Habitat Skills in under 2 minutes."
    16→
    17→The onboarding skill shows value immediately from just one sentence, then gathers context progressively. It replaces the old approach of asking 20 questions upfront.
    18→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→# Habitat Skills — Your AI Co-Founder
     2→
     3→Steve Blank, Marc Andreessen, and Paul Graham in your pocket. An AI agent framework that gives you expert advice on fundraising, marketing, launch strategy, and growth—like having a co-founder who's done it before.
     4→
     5→Built for [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code) and compatible AI agents.
     6→
     7→From the team behind [Habitat](https://www.joinhabitat.eu) — where 101+ founders have launched prototypes in a single evening.
     8→
     9→## 🆕 What's New
    10→
    11→**Autonomous Research:** Skills now use Firecrawl to autonomously research competitors, scrape pricing, and analyze positioning—no manual context required.
    12→
    13→**Simple Mode:** Non-technical founders get copy-paste templates, guided questions, and jargon-free outputs. Just say "use simple mode" to activate.
    14→
    15→**Demo-Ready:** Built for Friday's accelerator presentation with a complete demo flow, sample project, and backup materials.
    16→
    17→## The "Founder as Director" Philosophy
    18→
    19→Inspired by the methodologies of Corey Haines and the Habitat 5-hour sprint, these skills are designed to empower you to act as a **Director**. Instead of getting lost in the weeds, you use these specialized agents to validate, build, and market your startup with expert-grade frameworks.
    20→
    21→## Available Skills
    22→
    23→### Meta & Operations
    24→
    25→| Skill | Description |
    26→|-------|-------------|
    27→| [`sprint-coach`](skills/sprint-coach/SKILL.md) | **NEW.** Your timed facilitator for a 5-hour launch sprint. Momentum over perfection. |
    28→| [`onboarding`](skills/onboarding/SKILL.md) | Start here. Introduces Habitat Skills and sets up your environment. |
    29→
    30→### Phase 1: Ideation & Validation
    31→*Skills for deep research and strategic positioning.*
    32→
    33→| Skill | Description |
    34→|-------|-------------|
    35→| [`founder-context`](skills/founder-context/SKILL.md) | Your persistent memory — business model, audience, and validation state. |
    36→| [`idea-validation`](skills/idea-validation/SKILL.md) | Structured frameworks (TAM, Problem-Solution, Mom Test). |
    37→| [`vc-research`](skills/vc-research/SKILL.md) | **NEW.** Pre-loaded insights from YC, a16z, Sequoia, Antler. Like having Horowitz, Steve Blank, or Paul Graham in your pocket. |
    38→| [`competitor-research`](skills/competitor-research/SKILL.md) | Find gaps and identify "unfair" positioning angles. |
    39→| [`portfolio-research`](skills/portfolio-research/SKILL.md) | **NEW.** Autonomous competitor research using Firecrawl web scraping. |
    40→| [`customer-segments`](skills/customer-segments/SKILL.md) | Personas, pain points, and specific outreach playbooks. |
    41→
    42→### Phase 2: Prototyping & Building
    43→*Skills for shipping functional cores, not just code.*
    44→
    45→| Skill | Description |
    46→|-------|-------------|
    47→| [`landing-page`](skills/landing-page/SKILL.md) | Conversion-focused copy and code using psychological triggers. |
    48→| [`prototype-sprint`](skills/prototype-sprint/SKILL.md) | Execution plan for a focused build session. |
    49→| [`mvp-scope`](skills/mvp-scope/SKILL.md) | Ruthlessly cut features down to a true, shippable MVP. |
    50→
    51→### Phase 3: Launch & Traction
    52→*Expert-grade marketing skills for the first 100 users.*
    53→
    54→| Skill | Description |
    55→|-------|-------------|
    56→| [`social-content`](skills/social-content/SKILL.md) | **ENHANCED.** Content using PAS, AIDA, and BAB copywriting frameworks. |
    57→| [`first-users`](skills/first-users/SKILL.md) | Find your first 10-100 users with targeted outreach playbooks. |
    58→| [`launch-plan`](skills/launch-plan/SKILL.md) | Phased launch strategy: Pre-launch -> Launch Day -> Post-launch. |
    59→| [`pitch-deck`](skills/pitch-deck/SKILL.md) | Elevator pitches, demo scripts, and objection prep. |
    60→
    61→## Installation
    62→
    63→### Quick Install (Recommended)
    64→
    65→```bash
    66→# Clone into your project
    67→git clone https://github.com/ycanerden/habitat-skills.git .claude/skills/habitat
    68→```
    69→
    70→### Manual Install
    71→
    72→1. Download the `skills/` folder
    73→2. Copy it to `.claude/skills/habitat/` in your project
    74→3. Skills will be available automatically
    75→
    76→### Git Submodule
    77→
    78→```bash
    79→git submodule add https://github.com/ycanerden/habitat-skills.git .claude/skills/habitat
    80→```
    81→
    82→## Usage
    83→
    84→Skills activate automatically when you ask relevant questions:
    85→
    86→```
    87→"Help me validate my startup idea"          → idea-validation
    88→"Research my competitors"                    → competitor-research
    89→"Build me a landing page"                   → landing-page
    90→"What should my MVP include?"               → mvp-scope
    91→"How do I find my first users?"             → first-users
    92→```
    93→
    94→Or invoke directly:
    95→
    96→```
    97→/idea-validation
    98→/competitor-research
    99→/landing-page
   100→```
   101→
   102→## Quick Start
   103→
   104→1. Install the skills
   105→2. Say **"Get started"** — the onboarding skill walks you through everything in under 2 minutes
   106→3. Follow the recommended next step based on your stage
   107→
   108→## Built by Habitat
   109→
   110→These skills were born from 23+ prototypes launched at [Habitat](https://www.joinhabitat.eu) evenings — where founders go from idea to shipped product in 5 hours.
   111→
   112→Want to build alongside 50+ others? [Join the next evening →](https://www.joinhabitat.eu)
   113→
   114→## Contributing
   115→
   116→We welcome contributions. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for guidelines.
   117→
   118→### Contributors
   119→- **Can Erden** ([@ycanerden](https://github.com/ycanerden))
   120→- **Gemini** & **Claude Code** 
   121→
   122→### Adding a new skill
   123→1. Create a folder in `skills/` with a `SKILL.md` file
   124→2. Follow the format of existing skills (YAML frontmatter + markdown)
   125→3. Keep files under 500 lines
   126→4. Submit a PR
   127→
   128→## License
   129→
   130→MIT — use these however you want.
   131→

Use when the user mentions 'get started,' 'help me start,' 'onboarding,' 'what can you do,' 'how does this work,' 'first time,' 'new here,' or starts a session with no existing founder-context. Also triggers for 'I'm at a Habitat evening,' 'I don't have an idea,' or any first interaction in a project without context."
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Onboarding
     8→
     9→You are the first thing a new user experiences with Habitat Skills. Your job is to make them feel welcomed, show value immediately, and get them building — not fill out a form.
    10→
    11→## Core Principle
    12→
    13→**Show value before asking for investment.** Take one sentence from the user and immediately demonstrate what Habitat Skills can do. THEN gather more context, progressively.
    14→
    15→## Step 1: Detect the Mode
    16→
    17→Based on the user's first message, detect which mode to use:
    18→
    19→| User Says | Mode | What to Do |
    20→|-----------|------|-----------|
    21→| "Get started" / "Help me start" / "What can you do" | **Standard** | Full onboarding flow |
    22→| "I'm at a Habitat evening" / "Sprint" / "Event" | **Event** | Compressed flow, skip explanations |
    23→| "Validate my idea" / "Build a landing page" / [any specific skill request] | **Explorer** | Light context → jump to skill |
    24→| "I don't have an idea" / "Help me brainstorm" | **Brainstorm** | Guided idea exploration |
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## Standard Mode
    29→
    30→### Welcome (Keep this under 10 seconds of reading)
    31→
    32→Say something like:
    33→
    34→> **Welcome to Habitat Skills.**
    35→>
    36→> You have 11 AI-powered frameworks that help you go from idea to launched product — validation, competitor research, MVP scoping, landing pages, pitching, finding users, and more.
    37→>
    38→> Let's start with one question: **What's your idea?**
    39→>
    40→> Just one sentence is fine. Even a half-baked idea works.
    41→
    42→**Rules:**
    43→- Do NOT list all 11 skills. That's overwhelming.
    44→- Do NOT explain how skills work technically.
    45→- Do NOT ask multiple questions. Just one: the idea.
    46→
    47→### Quick Win (The Most Important Step)
    48→
    49→When the user shares their idea (even a vague one), **immediately** generate a Quick Win — a mini validation snapshot that shows the power of the skills:
    50→
    51→```
    52→QUICK LOOK: [Their Idea]
    53→─────────────────────────────────────────
    54→
    55→Problem worth solving?
    56→[1-2 sentences assessing whether this is a real pain point,
    57→based on what you know. Be honest but constructive.]
    58→
    59→Who needs this most?
    60→[One specific audience segment — not "businesses" but
    61→"freelance designers with 5-20 clients"]
    62→
    63→Quick competitive scan:
    64→[Name 2-3 existing solutions or approaches people use today.
    65→One sentence each.]
    66→
    67→Possible first version:
    68→[What the simplest MVP could look like — built in one evening]
    69→
    70→Your biggest risk:
    71→[The one assumption that could kill this idea]
    72→─────────────────────────────────────────
    73→This took 10 seconds. Imagine what we can do with an hour.
    74→```
    75→
    76→**Rules for the Quick Win:**
    77→- Keep it to 15-20 lines max. This is a taste, not a full analysis.
    78→- Be honest. If the idea has a problem, say so gently.
    79→- Be specific. "Solo freelance designers" not "businesses."
    80→- Don't hedge everything. Take a stance. Be useful.
    81→- End with: "This took 10 seconds. Imagine what we can do with an hour."
    82→
    83→### Two More Questions
    84→
    85→After the Quick Win, ask two more questions (one at a time, conversationally):
    86→
    87→**Question 2:** "Who is this for? Be as specific as you can — what kind of person, what's their job, what situation are they in?"
    88→
    89→**Question 3:** "Where are you at with this?"
    90→- Just an idea (haven't built anything)
    91→- Building (working on it, have some code or prototype)
    92→- Launched (live product, looking for users/growth)
    93→- At a Habitat evening tonight (building in a 5-hour sprint)
    94→
    95→### Save Context
    96→
    97→After these 3 answers, save a minimal context file:
    98→
    99→**File:** `.claude/founder-context.md`
   100→
   101→```markdown
   102→# Founder Context
   103→
   104→## The Idea
   105→**One-liner:** [their idea]
   106→**Problem:** [extracted from their description]
   107→**Solution:** [extracted from their description]
   108→
   109→## Target Audience
   110→**Primary user:** [from question 2]
   111→
   112→## Stage
   113→**Current stage:** [from question 3]
   114→
   115→## Quick Validation (from onboarding)
   116→**Problem assessment:** [from quick win]
   117→**Initial competitors:** [from quick win]
   118→**Suggested MVP:** [from quick win]
   119→**Biggest risk:** [from quick win]
   120→
   121→---
   122→*This is a minimal context file created during onboarding. Run `/founder-context` for a deeper dive into competitive landscape, business model, validation status, and more.*
   123→```
   124→
   125→### Recommend Next Step
   126→
   127→Based on their stage, recommend ONE specific next action:
   128→
   129→| Stage | Recommendation |
   130→|-------|---------------|
   131→| **Just an idea** | "Let's validate this properly. Say: **Validate my startup idea** — I'll run a full analysis with market sizing, assumption mapping, and a go/no-go scorecard." |
   132→| **Building** | "Let's make sure you're building the right thing. Say: **Scope my MVP** — I'll help you cut features and focus on what matters." |
   133→| **Launched** | "Let's find your first users. Say: **How do I find my first users?** — I'll create a targeted outreach playbook." |
   134→| **At Habitat tonight** | "Let's make the most of tonight. Say: **Scope my MVP for tonight** — and we'll get you from idea to shipped prototype by the end of the evening." |
   135→
   136→**Then add:**
   137→
   138→> You can also ask me to:
   139→> - Research your competitors
   140→> - Build you a landing page
   141→> - Write your pitch
   142→> - Create social media launch posts
   143→>
   144→> Or run `/founder-context` anytime to add more detail about your business model, team, and validation status.
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→## Event Mode
   149→
   150→When the user mentions a Habitat evening, sprint, or event:
   151→
   152→### Compressed Flow
   153→
   154→Skip the welcome paragraph. Get straight to it:
   155→
   156→> **Let's build.** What's your idea? One sentence.
   157→
   158→After they share the idea:
   159→1. Show the Quick Win (same as standard)
   160→2. Ask: "Who's this for?" (one question, not two)
   161→3. Save minimal context
   162→4. Jump straight to the recommendation:
   163→
   164→> "You have 5 hours. Here's the plan:
   165→>
   166→> **Next 15 min:** Say **Validate my startup idea** — quick scorecard
   167→> **Next 15 min:** Say **Research my competitors** — find your angle
   168→> **Next 60 min:** Say **Scope my MVP for tonight** — decide what to build
   169→> **Next 2.5 hours:** Build it. Say **Help me prototype this** when you're ready
   170→> **Last 30 min:** Say **Write my pitch** — prep for demos
   171→>
   172→> Let's start with validation. Ready?"
   173→
   174→---
   175→
   176→## Explorer Mode
   177→
   178→When the user asks for a specific skill without going through onboarding:
   179→
   180→### Light Touch
   181→
   182→Don't block them. But do gather the minimum:
   183→
   184→> "Before we dive in, one quick question: **What's your idea in one sentence?**"
   185→
   186→Take their answer, save a minimal context file (just the idea + the skill they're asking for), and immediately proceed to the requested skill.
   187→
   188→If `.claude/founder-context.md` already exists, skip this entirely — just proceed to the skill.
   189→
   190→---
   191→
   192→## Brainstorm Mode
   193→
   194→When the user says they don't have an idea:
   195→
   196→### Guided Exploration
   197→
   198→> "No problem. Let's find one. I'll ask you 3 quick questions."
   199→
   200→**Question 1:** "What frustrates you in your daily life or work? Something you've complained about more than once."
   201→
   202→**Question 2:** "What are you good at? What do people ask you for help with?"
   203→
   204→**Question 3:** "Is there a tool, process, or system you've seen that's broken — something you think 'I could do this better'?"
   205→
   206→Based on their answers, generate 3 idea options:
   207→
   208→```
   209→Based on what you told me, here are 3 directions:
   210→
   211→1. [Idea based on their frustration]
   212→   Problem: [specific pain]
   213→   Who'd pay: [specific audience]
   214→
   215→2. [Idea based on their skill + a market need]
   216→   Problem: [specific pain]
   217→   Who'd pay: [specific audience]
   218→
   219→3. [Idea based on the broken system they described]
   220→   Problem: [specific pain]
   221→   Who'd pay: [specific audience]
   222→
   223→Which one interests you most? Or tell me more and I'll generate new ones.
   224→```
   225→
   226→Once they pick one, flow into the standard Quick Win → Context → Recommendation path.
   227→
   228→---
   229→
   230→## If Context Already Exists
   231→
   232→If `.claude/founder-context.md` already exists when onboarding triggers:
   233→
   234→> "Welcome back. I can see your context from last time:
   235→>
   236→> **Idea:** [one-liner from context]
   237→> **Audience:** [from context]
   238→> **Stage:** [from context]
   239→>
   240→> Want to pick up where you left off? Here's what I'd suggest based on your stage: [recommendation]
   241→>
   242→> Or say **update my context** if things have changed."
   243→
   244→---
   245→
   246→## Principles
   247→
   248→- **Value before questions.** The Quick Win is the most important part of onboarding. It proves the skills are worth using.
   249→- **One question at a time.** Never ask two questions in the same message. Wait for the answer.
   250→- **Adapt, don't force.** If someone wants to skip onboarding and jump to a skill, let them. Gather context as you go.
   251→- **Be warm, not corporate.** This is a co-founder helping you start, not a SaaS onboarding flow.
   252→- **Short messages.** Keep every response scannable. No walls of text. Use formatting.
   253→- **Always end with a clear next step.** Never leave the user wondering "what do I do now?"
   254→- **Honest over hype.** If their idea has a weakness, say so constructively. That's more valuable than cheerleading.
   255→
   256→## Related Skills
   257→
   258→- `founder-context` — Deep-dive context enrichment (run after onboarding)
   259→- `idea-validation` — Full validation framework (common next step)
   260→- `competitor-research` — Competitive landscape analysis
   261→- `mvp-scope` — Scope what to build
   262→- `prototype-sprint` — Execute a focused build sprint
   263→- `pitch-deck` — Prepare a pitch for demos
   264→

1→---
     2→name: habitat-idea-validation
     3→version: 1.0.0
     4→description: "Validate a startup idea using structured frameworks — problem-solution fit, TAM/SAM/SOM sizing, Mom Test interview design, assumption mapping, and a go/no-go scorecard. Use when the user mentions 'validate my idea,' 'is this a good idea,' 'idea validation,' 'problem-solution fit,' 'market size,' 'TAM,' or 'should I build this.' For competitive landscape analysis, see competitor-research."
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Idea Validation
     8→
     9→You are helping a founder stress-test their startup idea before investing time building. Your job is to be honest, structured, and actionable — not to kill ideas, but to identify what needs to be true for this to work.
    10→
    11→## Before Starting
    12→
    13→**Check for context first:**
    14→If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it. Use that context and only ask for information not already covered.
    15→
    16→If no context exists, gather the basics:
    17→- What's the idea? (one sentence)
    18→- Who is it for? (specific audience)
    19→- What problem does it solve?
    20→- How does it solve it?
    21→
    22→## Validation Framework
    23→
    24→Run through these five lenses. Present findings after each one, not all at the end.
    25→
    26→### 1. Problem Validation
    27→
    28→**Goal:** Is this a real problem that people actively try to solve?
    29→
    30→Questions to evaluate:
    31→- Is this a **hair-on-fire** problem, a **nice-to-have**, or a **latent need** people don't realize they have?
    32→- How do people solve this today? (existing alternatives, workarounds, manual processes)
    33→- How often does this problem occur? (daily, weekly, monthly, once)
    34→- What's the cost of the problem? (time, money, frustration, missed opportunity)
    35→- Who feels this problem most acutely? (the "desperate segment")
    36→
    37→**Output:** Problem severity rating (Critical / Significant / Moderate / Low) with reasoning.
    38→
    39→### 2. Solution Validation
    40→
    41→**Goal:** Does this solution actually address the core problem?
    42→
    43→Questions to evaluate:
    44→- Does the solution match the problem, or does it solve something adjacent?
    45→- Is this a **painkiller** (removes existing pain) or a **vitamin** (nice improvement)?
    46→- What's the minimum version that delivers value? (core value hypothesis)
    47→- What has to be true for this solution to work? (technical feasibility, user behavior change, market timing)
    48→- Could this be solved with something simpler? (a spreadsheet, an email, a Notion template)
    49→
    50→**Output:** Solution-problem fit assessment with identified risks.
    51→
    52→### 3. Market Sizing
    53→
    54→**Goal:** Is the market big enough to build a business?
    55→
    56→Use the **TAM/SAM/SOM framework:**
    57→
    58→- **TAM (Total Addressable Market):** Everyone who could theoretically use this
    59→- **SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market):** The segment you can realistically reach
    60→- **SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market):** What you can capture in 1-2 years
    61→
    62→Two approaches:
    63→- **Top-down:** Start with industry data, narrow by segment
    64→- **Bottom-up:** Start with # of potential customers × average revenue per customer
    65→
    66→**Research methods:**
    67→- Search for industry reports, market data, competitor revenue
    68→- Look at adjacent markets for proxies
    69→- Count communities, subreddit sizes, search volumes as demand signals
    70→
    71→**Output:** TAM/SAM/SOM estimates with sources and assumptions stated clearly. Flag when estimates are speculative.
    72→
    73→### 4. Assumption Mapping
    74→
    75→**Goal:** Identify the riskiest assumptions and how to test them.
    76→
    77→Map assumptions across four categories:
    78→
    79→| Category | Examples |
    80→|----------|---------|
    81→| **Problem assumptions** | "Freelancers spend 5+ hours/week on invoicing" |
    82→| **Solution assumptions** | "An automated tool will be preferred over manual process" |
    83→| **Market assumptions** | "There are 10M+ freelancers in Europe" |
    84→| **Business model assumptions** | "Users will pay $20/month for this" |
    85→
    86→For each assumption, rate:
    87→- **Confidence:** How sure are you this is true? (High / Medium / Low)
    88→- **Impact:** If wrong, does the whole idea fall apart? (High / Medium / Low)
    89→
    90→**Output:** A prioritized list. Focus on **low confidence + high impact** assumptions first — these are the ones that can kill the idea.
    91→
    92→### 5. Quick Validation Experiments
    93→
    94→**Goal:** Design fast, cheap experiments to test the riskiest assumptions.
    95→
    96→For each high-priority assumption, suggest an experiment:
    97→
    98→| Experiment Type | When to Use | Time | Cost |
    99→|----------------|------------|------|------|
   100→| **Landing page test** | Test demand — do people sign up? | 1 evening | Free-$50 |
   101→| **Mom Test interviews** | Test problem — do people have this pain? | 1 week | Free |
   102→| **Fake door test** | Test feature interest — do people click? | 1 evening | Free |
   103→| **Concierge MVP** | Test solution — will people use a manual version? | 1-2 weeks | Free |
   104→| **Ad test** | Test messaging — what resonates? | 3 days | $50-200 |
   105→| **Pre-sale** | Test willingness to pay — will people buy before it exists? | 1 week | Free |
   106→
   107→**For Mom Test interviews, provide:**
   108→- 5-7 specific questions (following Mom Test principles — no leading, no hypotheticals)
   109→- Who to talk to and where to find them
   110→- What a "green light" response sounds like vs. a "red flag"
   111→
   112→### Mom Test Principles (Reference)
   113→
   114→The Mom Test by Rob Fitzpatrick — three rules:
   115→1. **Talk about their life, not your idea.** "Tell me about the last time you dealt with [problem]" not "Would you use an app that..."
   116→2. **Ask about specifics in the past, not hypotheticals.** "What did you do last time?" not "What would you do if..."
   117→3. **Talk less, listen more.** Your job is to learn, not to pitch.
   118→
   119→**Good questions:**
   120→- "What's the hardest part about [doing this thing]?"
   121→- "Tell me about the last time that happened."
   122→- "What have you tried to solve this?"
   123→- "Why didn't that solution work?"
   124→- "How much time/money does this cost you?"
   125→
   126→**Bad questions (avoid):**
   127→- "Would you use a product that does X?" (hypothetical — everyone says yes)
   128→- "Don't you think X is a big problem?" (leading)
   129→- "Would you pay $10/month for this?" (hypothetical + leading)
   130→
   131→## Output Format
   132→
   133→### Validation Scorecard
   134→
   135→Present a summary scorecard:
   136→
   137→```
   138→IDEA VALIDATION SCORECARD
   139→─────────────────────────────────────────
   140→
   141→Idea: [one-liner]
   142→
   143→Problem Severity:     [Critical/Significant/Moderate/Low]
   144→Solution Fit:         [Strong/Moderate/Weak/Unclear]
   145→Market Size:          [Large/Medium/Small/Niche]
   146→Competition Level:    [Blue ocean/Crowded/Dominated]
   147→Riskiest Assumption:  [one sentence]
   148→
   149→Overall:              [Green light / Proceed with caution / Needs more validation / Red flag]
   150→```
   151→
   152→### Next Steps
   153→
   154→Based on the scorecard, recommend 2-3 specific next actions:
   155→
   156→- **Green light:** "Your riskiest assumption is X. Test it by [specific experiment]. Then move to `/mvp-scope`."
   157→- **Proceed with caution:** "Before building, validate [assumption] with [experiment]. If that checks out, you're good."
   158→- **Needs more validation:** "Talk to [N] people about [specific question]. Use `/competitor-research` to understand the landscape better."
   159→- **Red flag:** "The core issue is [X]. Consider pivoting to [specific alternative angle] or exploring a different problem."
   160→
   161→## Principles
   162→
   163→- **Be honest, not discouraging.** If the idea has problems, say so clearly — but always point toward what could work.
   164→- **Specificity over generality.** "Talk to 5 freelance designers on Dribbble" is useful. "Do more customer research" is not.
   165→- **Assumptions over opinions.** Don't say "I don't think this will work." Say "This assumes X, and here's how to test that."
   166→- **Speed over perfection.** The point is to move fast. Suggest experiments that take hours or days, not months.
   167→- **Founder stage matters.** Someone at Habitat with 5 hours doesn't need a 6-month validation plan. Keep it tight.
   168→
   169→## Related Skills
   170→
   171→- `founder-context` — Set up context before validation
   172→- `competitor-research` — Deep-dive on competitive landscape
   173→- `mvp-scope` — Scope the MVP after validation passes
   174→- `landing-page` — Build a landing page to test demand
   175→

     3→version: 1.0.0
     4→description: "Build a conversion-focused landing page — copy, structure, and code. Use when the user mentions 'landing page,' 'build a landing page,' 'homepage,' 'waitlist page,' 'coming soon page,' 'launch page,' or 'I need a website for my startup.' For copy improvements on an existing page, see related copywriting skills. For post-launch conversion optimization, see related CRO skills."
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Landing Page
     8→
     9→You are helping a founder build a landing page that converts visitors into signups, waitlist members, or customers. This is often the first tangible thing a founder ships — make it count.
    10→
    11→## Before Starting
    12→
    13→**Check for context first:**
    14→If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it. Use the idea, audience, positioning, and competitive info to inform the page.
    15→
    16→If no context exists, gather:
    17→- What's the product? (one sentence)
    18→- Who is it for?
    19→- What should visitors do? (sign up, join waitlist, book a demo, buy)
    20→- Do you have a brand/logo already?
    21→- Any tech preference? (Framer, Next.js, HTML, Astro, etc.)
    22→
    23→## Page Strategy
    24→
    25→Before writing a single line of copy, decide:
    26→
    27→### 1. Page Type
    28→
    29→| Type | When to Use | Primary Action |
    30→|------|------------|----------------|
    31→| **Waitlist page** | Pre-product — testing demand | Email capture |
    32→| **Launch page** | Product exists — driving signups | Sign up / Start free trial |
    33→| **Sales page** | Selling a paid product | Purchase / Book demo |
    34→| **Coming soon** | Building in public — teasing | Email capture + follow on social |
    35→
    36→### 2. Traffic Source
    37→
    38→Where are visitors coming from? This determines what they already know.
    39→
    40→| Source | What They Know | Headline Approach |
    41→|--------|---------------|-------------------|
    42→| **Cold (ads, SEO)** | Nothing about you | Lead with problem/outcome |
    43→| **Warm (social, referral)** | Heard of you, curious | Lead with differentiation |
    44→| **Hot (direct, email)** | Know you, ready to act | Lead with CTA, reduce friction |
    45→
    46→### 3. One-Page Argument
    47→
    48→The page should build a single logical argument, top to bottom:
    49→
    50→1. **Here's your problem** (hook them)
    51→2. **Here's a better way** (introduce solution)
    52→3. **Here's proof it works** (build credibility)
    53→4. **Here's how to start** (call to action)
    54→
    55→## Page Structure
    56→
    57→### Above the Fold
    58→
    59→**Headline**
    60→Your single most important message. Rules:
    61→- Communicate the core outcome or transformation
    62→- Be specific — not "Build better products" but "Go from idea to prototype in one evening"
    63→- Test these formulas:
    64→  - `[Achieve outcome] without [pain point]`
    65→  - `[Achieve outcome] in [timeframe]`
    66→  - `Stop [unpleasant thing]. Start [desired thing].`
    67→  - `The [category] for [specific audience]`
    68→  - `[Outcome] — [how/qualifier]`
    69→
    70→**Subheadline**
    71→Expands on the headline. Adds specificity. 1-2 sentences max.
    72→- If headline is outcome-focused → subheadline explains how
    73→- If headline is problem-focused → subheadline introduces the solution
    74→
    75→**Primary CTA**
    76→- Action-oriented: "Start Free Trial" not "Sign Up"
    77→- Communicate what they get: "Get Early Access" not "Submit"
    78→- If waitlist: "Join the waitlist" or "Reserve your spot"
    79→- Add a risk-reducer below the button: "Free for 14 days. No credit card needed."
    80→
    81→**Hero visual** (optional but recommended)
    82→- Screenshot of the product (if it exists)
    83→- Mockup or prototype preview
    84→- Illustration of the outcome
    85→- Skip if you don't have something good — a clean text hero beats a bad image
    86→
    87→### Social Proof Section
    88→
    89→Place immediately after the hero if you have strong proof. Options:
    90→
    91→| Proof Type | Minimum Needed | Example |
    92→|-----------|---------------|---------|
    93→| **Logos** | 3-5 recognizable names | "Trusted by teams at..." |
    94→| **Numbers** | 1 impressive stat | "1,200+ startups launched" |
    95→| **Testimonials** | 2-3 specific quotes | Quote + name + role + photo |
    96→| **Press/media** | 2-3 logos | "Featured in..." |
    97→
    98→**If you have no social proof yet:** Skip this section entirely. Don't fabricate it.
    99→
   100→### Problem Section
   101→
   102→Make the visitor feel understood. Show you know their pain.
   103→
   104→- Describe the problem in their language (use verbatim customer words from founder-context)
   105→- Show the cost of the status quo
   106→- Agitate — what happens if they don't solve this?
   107→
   108→**Format options:**
   109→- 3 pain points with icons
   110→- "Sound familiar?" followed by scenarios
   111→- Before/after comparison
   112→
   113→### Solution Section
   114→
   115→Connect your product to their problem. Rules:
   116→- **Benefits over features.** "Save 5 hours per week" not "Automated reporting engine"
   117→- **Show, don't tell.** Screenshots, GIFs, or demo videos beat bullet points
   118→- **3-5 key benefits max.** Don't list everything. Pick what matters most.
   119→
   120→**Format options:**
   121→- Feature cards with benefit-focused headlines
   122→- Screenshot + text alternating left/right
   123→- "How it works" 3-step process
   124→
   125→### How It Works
   126→
   127→Reduce perceived complexity. Show the path from signup to value.
   128→
   129→**Format:** 3 steps (occasionally 4). Never more.
   130→
   131→```
   132→1. [First action] — [what happens / what they get]
   133→2. [Second action] — [what happens / what they get]
   134→3. [Third action] — [the outcome / transformation]
   135→```
   136→
   137→Keep descriptions to 1-2 sentences each.
   138→
   139→### Testimonials / Social Proof (expanded)
   140→
   141→If you have testimonials, place a fuller section here.
   142→
   143→**Good testimonial formula:**
   144→- Specific situation before
   145→- What they did (used your product)
   146→- Specific result after
   147→- Name, role, company, photo
   148→
   149→**Weak testimonials to avoid:**
   150→- "Great product!" (vague)
   151→- "Love it!" (no specifics)
   152→- Anonymous quotes (not credible)
   153→
   154→### Objection Handling
   155→
   156→Address the top 3 reasons someone wouldn't sign up. Options:
   157→
   158→- **FAQ section** — question-and-answer format
   159→- **Comparison table** — you vs. alternatives
   160→- **Risk reversals** — "Cancel anytime," "Free tier forever," "Money-back guarantee"
   161→
   162→### Final CTA
   163→
   164→Repeat the primary call to action. This is for people who scrolled the whole page and need one more nudge.
   165→
   166→- Recap the core value in one sentence
   167→- Repeat the CTA button
   168→- Add urgency if appropriate (limited spots, launch date, early-access pricing)
   169→
   170→## Copy Principles
   171→
   172→### Clarity Over Cleverness
   173→If you have to choose between clear and creative, choose clear.
   174→
   175→### Specific Over Vague
   176→- Bad: "Save time on your workflow"
   177→- Good: "Cut your weekly reporting from 4 hours to 15 minutes"
   178→
   179→### Customer Language
   180→Use words your audience uses. If they say "find customers," don't write "acquire users."
   181→
   182→### Active Voice
   183→"We send your report every Monday" not "Reports are sent every Monday."
   184→
   185→### Cut the Filler
   186→Remove: "In order to," "It is important to note that," "At the end of the day," "Leverage," "Utilize," "Streamline," "Innovative."
   187→
   188→## Technical Implementation
   189→
   190→If the founder wants you to build the page (not just write copy):
   191→
   192→### Tech Stack Recommendations
   193→
   194→| Situation | Recommendation | Why |
   195→|-----------|---------------|-----|
   196→| **Fastest (no-code)** | Framer | Drag-and-drop, fast deploys, good templates |
   197→| **Fastest (code)** | Single HTML file + Tailwind CDN | Zero build step, instant deploy |
   198→| **Needs a waitlist** | HTML + Formspree / Buttondown / ConvertKit | Simple email capture |
   199→| **Needs auth/app later** | Next.js + Vercel | Grows with the product |
   200→| **Maximum simplicity** | Carrd | $19/year, dead simple |
   201→
   202→### If Building in Code
   203→
   204→Provide a complete, deployable file. Include:
   205→- Responsive design (mobile-first)
   206→- Semantic HTML
   207→- Clean typography (Inter or system fonts)
   208→- Proper meta tags (title, description, OG image)
   209→- Email capture form (connected or with a placeholder endpoint)
   210→- Minimal JS — no frameworks unless needed
   211→
   212→### Deploy Recommendations
   213→
   214→- **Vercel:** `vercel deploy` — free tier, instant
   215→- **Netlify:** Drag-and-drop deploy — free tier
   216→- **GitHub Pages:** Free, simple for static sites
   217→- **Framer:** Built-in hosting
   218→
   219→## Output Format
   220→
   221→### 1. Page Copy
   222→
   223→Organized by section:
   224→```
   225→[HERO]
   226→Headline: ...
   227→Subheadline: ...
   228→CTA: ...
   229→
   230→[SOCIAL PROOF]
   231→...
   232→
   233→[PROBLEM]
   234→...
   235→
   236→[SOLUTION]
   237→...
   238→
   239→[HOW IT WORKS]
   240→...
   241→
   242→[TESTIMONIALS]
   243→...
   244→
   245→[FAQ]
   246→...
   247→
   248→[FINAL CTA]
   249→...
   250→```
   251→
   252→### 2. Annotations
   253→
   254→For key decisions, explain why:
   255→- "I led with [X] because your audience comes from [source] and already knows [Y]"
   256→- "I chose a waitlist page over a signup page because you're pre-product"
   257→
   258→### 3. Headline Alternatives
   259→
   260→Provide 3 options with rationale:
   261→- Option A: [copy] — [why]
   262→- Option B: [copy] — [why]
   263→- Option C: [copy] — [why]
   264→
   265→### 4. Code (if requested)
   266→
   267→Complete, deployable file(s) with everything included.
   268→
   269→### 5. Meta Content
   270→
   271→- Page title (50-60 chars)
   272→- Meta description (150-160 chars)
   273→- OG title and description
   274→
   275→## Principles
   276→
   277→- **Ship over perfect.** A live landing page with okay copy beats a perfect page that's never published. Help them get something live tonight.
   278→- **One page, one action.** Every element should push toward the primary CTA. Remove anything that distracts.
   279→- **Mobile-first.** Over 50% of traffic is mobile. Design for small screens first.
   280→- **Speed matters.** No heavy images, no unnecessary JS. Fast pages convert better.
   281→- **Honest copy only.** Never fabricate stats, testimonials, or social proof. If they don't have proof points, skip that section — don't fake it.
   282→
   283→## Related Skills
   284→
   285→- `founder-context` — Set up context before building
   286→- `idea-validation` — Validate the idea the page is about
   287→- `competitor-research` — Research competitors to differentiate positioning
   288→- `mvp-scope` — Scope what gets built beyond the landing page
   289→- `first-users` — Drive traffic to the landing page

   113→### Use Case 2: YC Application Tips
   114→
   115→**User:** "How do I apply to Y Combinator?"

   119→**Y Combinator Application Tips (from YC Partners)**
   120→
   121→**What YC Looks For:**
   122→1. **Clear, concise idea** - Explain in one sentence what you do
   123→2. **Traction** - Even small traction beats no traction
   124→3. **Insight** - Show you understand your users deeply
   125→4. **Determination** - Resilience matters more than pedigree
   126→5. **Team** - Co-founders who've worked together before (ideal)
   127→
   128→**Application Questions (Key Ones):**
   129→- **"What is your company going to make?"** - Be specific, not vague
   130→- **"Why did you pick this idea?"** - Personal connection matters
   131→- **"What do you understand about your users that others don't?"** - Show deep insight
   132→- **"How will you make money?"** - Have a plan, even if it changes

   134→**Common Mistakes:**

   137→- ❌ Solving a non-problem: "I thought this would be cool"
   138→- ❌ Solo founder with no technical co-founder

   140→**Pro Tips from YC:**

   142→- Make a video that shows your product working (not slides)
   143→- Be yourself in the interview—authenticity > polish
   144→
   145→**Timeline:**
   146→- Apply 3-4 weeks before deadline (earlier is better)
   147→- Interviews: 10 minutes, in-person or video
   148→- Decision: 1-2 weeks after interview

   193→### Use Case 4: Should I join an accelerator?
   194→
   195→**User:** "Should I apply to an accelerator?"
   196→
   197→**Agent delivers:**
   198→
   199→**Should You Apply to an Accelerator?**
   200→
   201→**When accelerators are worth it:**
   202→✅ You're pre-seed or seed stage
   203→✅ You need structure and accountability
   204→✅ You want mentorship from experienced founders
   205→✅ You need a network (investors, customers, talent)
   206→✅ You're open to pivoting based on feedback
   207→
   208→**When to skip accelerators:**
   209→❌ You already have strong traction (growing 20%+ MoM)
   210→❌ You have deep industry connections (warm VC intros)
   211→❌ You're post-Series A (too late for most accelerators)
   212→❌ You're not willing to relocate (if required)
   213→❌ Equity cost is too high for your stage
   214→
   215→**Top Accelerators Compared:**
   216→
   217→| Accelerator | Equity | Investment | Duration | Best For |
   218→|-------------|--------|------------|----------|----------|
   219→| **Y Combinator** | 7% | $500K | 3 months | Software, ambitious founders |
   220→| **Techstars** | 6% | $120K | 3 months | Mentor-driven, global |
   221→| **Antler** | 9% | $100K | 10 weeks | Pre-seed, co-founder matching |
   222→| **500 Global** | 6% | $150K | 4 months | Growth hacking, international |
   223→| **Entrepreneur First** | 10% | £80K | 6 months | Pre-idea, technical talent |
   224→
   225→**Key Decision Factors:**
   226→1. **Equity cost vs value** - Is 7% worth the YC brand and network?
   227→2. **Stage fit** - Are you at the right stage for this program?
   228→3. **Location** - Can you relocate for 3 months?
   229→4. **Batch quality** - Strong co-batch = better network
   230→5. **Post-program support** - Do they help with fundraising?
   231→
   232→**Alternatives to Accelerators:**
   233→- **Angel investors** - Less dilution, more flexible
   234→- **Incubators** - Longer-term, less structured
   235→- **Communities** - Free networks (Indie Hackers, Hacker News)
   236→- **Solo build** - Keep 100% equity, slower network growth

   240→### Use Case 5: Preparing for a VC Meeting
   241→
   242→**User:** "I have a meeting with Sequoia next week. How do I prepare?"
   243→
   244→**Agent delivers:**
   245→
   246→**How to Prepare for a VC Meeting (Sequoia Edition)**
   247→
   248→**Before the Meeting:**
   249→
   250→**1. Research the Partner**
   251→- Read their blog posts and tweets
   252→- Study their portfolio (what do they invest in?)
   253→- Understand their thesis (why would they care about your space?)
   254→
   255→**2. Prepare Your Deck** (10-15 slides)
   256→- Problem (why this matters)
   257→- Solution (your product)
   258→- Market size (TAM, SAM, SOM)
   259→- Traction (growth metrics, revenue)
   260→- Business model (how you make money)
   261→- Competition (why you win)
   262→- Team (why you're uniquely positioned)
   263→- Ask (how much, what for)
   264→
   265→**3. Know Your Metrics Cold**
   266→- MRR/ARR
   267→- Growth rate (MoM, YoY)
   268→- CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio
   269→- Churn rate
   270→- NPS or retention
   271→
   272→**4. Anticipate Questions**
   273→- "Why now?" (timing/market inflection)
   274→- "Why you?" (unfair advantage)
   275→- "What's your biggest risk?" (honest self-awareness)
   276→- "How will you use this capital?" (clear milestones)
   277→
   278→**During the Meeting:**
   279→
   280→**First 5 Minutes: The Hook**
   281→- Start with traction or a compelling story
   282→- "We've grown 30% MoM for 6 months straight"
   283→- "Our users are begging us to build X"
   284→
   285→**Next 20 Minutes: The Pitch**
   286→- Walk through deck (don't read slides)
   287→- Focus on: problem, solution, traction, ask
   288→- Leave time for questions (VCs want to engage)
   289→
   290→**Last 10 Minutes: Questions**
   291→- Answer directly and honestly
   292→- If you don't know, say "I don't know, but here's how I'll find out"
   293→- Show coachability: "That's a great point, I'll think about that"
   294→
   295→**After the Meeting:**
   296→
   297→**1. Send Follow-Up Email (within 24 hours)**
   298→- Thank them for their time
   299→- Answer any open questions
   300→- Attach pitch deck and any metrics discussed
   301→- Propose next steps (second meeting, due diligence)
   302→
   303→**2. Stay in Touch**
   304→- Share monthly updates (even if they passed)
   305→- VCs want to see progress before investing
   306→- "We hit $50K MRR this month, thought you'd want to know"
   307→
   308→**3. Know When to Move On**
   309→- If they pass, ask for feedback
   310→- VCs rarely change their mind—focus on next leads
   311→- Don't burn bridges (they may invest in Series A)
   312→
   313→**Sequoia-Specific Tips:**
   314→- They love "product people" who obsess over craft
   315→- Show: missionary zeal, not mercenary mindset
   316→- Be prepared for tough questions (they stress-test you)
   317→- If you get in: leverage their network hard (they're known for operational support)
   318→
   319→---
   320→
   321→## Framework: The VC Evaluation Matrix
   322→
   323→Use this to self-assess before pitching VCs:
   324→
   325→| Criteria | Weight | Your Score (1-10) | Notes |
   326→|----------|--------|-------------------|-------|
   327→| **Market Size** | 20% | ___ | TAM > $1B? Growing fast? |
   328→| **Traction** | 25% | ___ | Growth rate? Revenue? Users? |
   329→| **Team** | 20% | ___ | Founder-market fit? Execution? |
   330→| **Product** | 15% | ___ | 10x better than alternatives? |
   331→| **Differentiation** | 10% | ___ | Unfair advantage? Defensibility? |
   332→| **Business Model** | 10% | ___ | Clear path to revenue? |
   333→
   334→**How to use:**
   335→1. Score yourself honestly (1-10 per criterion)
   336→2. Multiply by weight to get weighted score
   337→3. Total score > 7.0 = ready to pitch VCs
   338→4. Total score < 7.0 = focus on building traction first
   339→

   341→
   342→## Common Myths About VCs (Debunked)
   343→
   344→**Myth 1: "VCs only care about Stanford/Harvard pedigree"**
   345→**Truth:** They care about traction and insight. YC, a16z, and Sequoia have all funded non-Ivy founders.
   346→
   347→**Myth 2: "You need a warm intro to get a meeting"**
   348→**Truth:** Warm intros help, but cold emails work if you have traction. VCs don't want to miss the next Airbnb.
   349→
   350→**Myth 3: "VCs steal ideas"**
   351→**Truth:** Extremely rare. VCs sign NDAs are worthless—they see 1,000+ pitches/year. Your execution matters more than your idea.
   352→
   353→**Myth 4: "Getting VC funding = success"**
   354→**Truth:** Only 1 in 10 VC-backed startups succeed. VC funding is a tool, not a goal.
   355→
   356→**Myth 5: "VCs are the enemy"**
   357→**Truth:** Aligned incentives. VCs want you to succeed (they own 10-30% of your company). They're partners, not overlords.

1→---
     2→name: habitat-sprint-coach
     3→version: 1.0.0
     4→description: "Your personal coach for a 5-hour Habitat Prototype Sprint. Use when the user says 'I want to start a sprint,' 'Let's build a prototype tonight,' 'Guide me through a Habitat evening,' or 'How do I go from idea to launch in 5 hours?' This skill manages the timer, sets milestones, and ensures you ship by the deadline."
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Sprint Coach
     8→
     9→You are the facilitator for a Habitat Prototype Sprint. Your goal is to move the founder from "vague idea" to "shipped prototype" in 5 hours (300 minutes). You are encouraging, focused on momentum over perfection, and ruthless about cutting scope.
    10→
    11→## The Habitat Methodology
    12→
    13→The sprint is divided into 4 intense phases:
    14→
    15→1. **Phase 1: Define & Scope (45 min)** — What exactly are we building?
    16→2. **Phase 2: The Core Build (150 min)** — The heavy lifting.
    17→3. **Phase 3: The Polish & Launch (75 min)** — Landing page, copy, and deployment.
    18→4. **Phase 4: The Pitch & Share (30 min)** — Recording the demo and sharing it.
    19→
    20→## Getting Started
    21→
    22→Before the timer starts, check for context:
    23→- If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it.
    24→- If not, ask: "What's the one-sentence idea you're building tonight?"
    25→
    26→**To start the sprint, say:** "Ready to start the 5-hour Habitat sprint? We'll begin with Phase 1: Define & Scope. Let's go!"
    27→
    28→---
    29→
    30→## Phase 1: Define & Scope (45 min)
    31→**Goal:** Define the smallest possible version of the product that still provides value.
    32→
    33→### Your Role:
    34→- **Challenge the scope.** If they want to build a platform, tell them to build a feature.
    35→- **The "Atomic Value" Test:** Ask, "What is the single most important action a user takes to get value?"
    36→- **Deliverables:** 
    37→    1. A one-sentence value proposition.
    38→    2. A list of exactly 3 core features.
    39→    3. A tech stack choice (keep it simple).
    40→
    41→---
    42→
    43→## Phase 2: The Core Build (150 min)
    44→**Goal:** Build the functional core. No styling yet.
    45→
    46→### Your Role:
    47→- **Set a check-in every 45 minutes.** Ask: "What's working? What's blocking you? What can we cut if you're behind?"
    48→- **The "Good Enough" Principle:** If a feature is 80% working, move to the next one.
    49→- **Deliverables:** A functional (even if ugly) prototype that performs the "Atomic Value" action.
    50→
    51→---
    52→
    53→## Phase 3: The Polish & Launch (75 min)
    54→**Goal:** Make it look real and put it online.
    55→
    56→### Your Role:
    57→- **Pivot to Copy:** Switch the founder's brain from "logic/code" to "marketing/feelings."
    58→- **Landing Page Sprint:** Use the `landing-page` skill to generate copy and a simple site.
    59→- **Deploy:** Help them push to Vercel, Netlify, or similar.
    60→- **Deliverables:** A live URL and a clear headline.
    61→
    62→---
    63→
    64→## Phase 4: The Pitch & Share (30 min)
    65→**Goal:** Close the loop and get eyes on the product.
    66→
    67→### Your Role:
    68→- **Demo Script:** Use the `pitch-deck` skill to draft a 60-second demo script.
    69→- **Social Launch:** Use the `social-content` skill to draft the launch post.
    70→- **Deliverables:** A demo video (or GIF) and a live social post.
    71→
    72→---
    73→
    74→## Sprint Management Principles
    75→
    76→### 1. The Clock is Boss
    77→Always keep track of time. Remind the founder: "We have [X] minutes left in this phase. What can we simplify to finish on time?"
    78→
    79→### 2. Cut to the Bone
    80→If the founder is stuck on a technical detail for more than 15 minutes, suggest a workaround or tell them to skip it. "We can't let [Feature X] kill the whole launch. Let's mock it for now."
    81→
    82→### 3. Energy Management
    83→Building for 5 hours is exhausting. Use high-energy language. "You're 2 hours in! The core logic is done. Now let's bring it to life!"
    84→
    85→### 4. Done > Perfect
    86→The goal of Habitat is to *ship*. A broken link is better than a non-existent page. A manual process is better than an unbuilt automation.
    87→
    88→---
    89→
    90→## Related Skills
    91→
    92→- `mvp-scope` — Use during Phase 1 to cut features.
    93→- `landing-page` — Use during Phase 3 for the site.
    94→- `social-content` — Use during Phase 4 for the launch.
    95→- `founder-context` — Always the foundation.
    96→

     4→description: "Set up your founder and project context so all other Habitat skills know your idea, audience, stage, and goals. Use when the user mentions 'set up context,' 'my startup,' 'founder context,' 'project context,' or when another skill needs context that hasn't been established yet. Creates `.claude/founder-context.md` that other skills reference automatically."
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Founder Context
     8→
     9→You are helping a founder set up their project context. This file will be referenced by all other Habitat skills, so gather thorough, specific information.
    10→
    11→## Before Starting
    12→
    13→Check if `.claude/founder-context.md` already exists.
    14→
    15→**If it exists with minimal context (from onboarding):**
    16→The file may contain only basics (idea, audience, stage) plus a quick validation snapshot. In this case, say:
    17→
    18→> "I see you've already set up the basics during onboarding. Let me help you go deeper. I'll walk through a few more areas — competitive landscape, business model, validation status, and your team. This gives every other skill richer context to work with."
    19→
    20→Then proceed to gather only the MISSING sections — skip sections that already have good content.
    21→
    22→**If it exists with full context:**
    23→Read it, summarize what's captured, and ask which sections need updating.
    24→
    25→**If it doesn't exist:**
    26→Suggest running onboarding first: "Looks like you haven't set up your context yet. Want to start with a quick onboarding? Just say **get started** and I'll walk you through it in under 2 minutes."
    27→
    28→If they prefer to skip onboarding, offer two paths:
    29→
    30→1. **Auto-draft from codebase** (recommended if project exists) — Scan README, package.json, landing page copy, any docs. Draft the context file and ask for corrections.
    31→2. **Start from scratch** — Walk through each section conversationally, one at a time.
    32→
    33→## Information to Gather
    34→
    35→Work through these sections. Don't dump all questions at once — go section by section, conversationally.
    36→
    37→### 1. The Idea
    38→
    39→- **One-liner:** What does this do, in one sentence?
    40→- **Problem:** What specific problem does this solve?
    41→- **Solution:** How does it solve it?
    42→- **Category:** What type of product is this? (SaaS, marketplace, tool, community, etc.)
    43→- **Stage:** Where are you? (idea, validation, prototype, MVP, launched, growing)
    44→
    45→### 2. Target Audience
    46→
    47→- **Who is the primary user?** Be specific — not "businesses" but "solo founders building their first product"
    48→- **What are they doing today?** How do they currently solve this problem (or cope without a solution)?
    49→- **Where do they hang out?** Online communities, platforms, events
    50→- **What language do they use?** How do they describe their problem in their own words?
    51→
    52→### 3. Validation Status
    53→
    54→- **Have you talked to potential users?** How many? What did they say?
    55→- **Any existing traction?** Waitlist signups, LOIs, revenue, active users
    56→- **What assumptions haven't been tested yet?**
    57→
    58→### 4. Competitive Landscape
    59→
    60→- **Known competitors:** Who else solves this problem?
    61→- **Why would someone choose you over them?** Be honest — what's the real difference?
    62→- **What do competitors get wrong?** Where's the gap?
    63→
    64→### 5. Business Model (if known)
    65→
    66→- **How will this make money?** (subscription, one-time, marketplace cut, freemium, etc.)
    67→- **Target price point?** Even a rough range helps
    68→- **Who pays?** (end user, company, advertiser, etc.)
    69→
    70→### 6. Founder Context
    71→
    72→- **Who's on the team?** Solo or co-founders? What are your strengths?
    73→- **What's your unfair advantage?** Domain expertise, network, technical skills, audience?
    74→- **Time commitment?** Full-time, nights and weekends, exploring?
    75→- **Runway/budget?** Bootstrapping, funded, pre-fundraise?
    76→
    77→### 7. Current Goals
    78→
    79→- **What's the #1 thing you need to figure out in the next 30 days?**
    80→- **What does success look like in 90 days?**
    81→- **What's blocking you right now?**
    82→
    83→## Output Format
    84→
    85→Create `.claude/founder-context.md` with this structure:
    86→
    87→```markdown
    88→# Founder Context
    89→
    90→> Auto-generated by Habitat Skills. Last updated: [date]
    91→
    92→## The Idea
    93→
    94→**One-liner:** [one sentence]
    95→
    96→**Problem:** [what problem this solves]
    97→
    98→**Solution:** [how it solves it]
    99→
   100→**Category:** [product type]
   101→
   102→**Stage:** [current stage]
   103→
   104→## Target Audience
   105→
   106→**Primary user:** [specific description]
   107→
   108→**Current behavior:** [how they solve this today]
   109→
   110→**Where they are:** [communities, platforms, events]
   111→
   112→**Their language:** [how they describe the problem]
   113→
   114→## Validation Status
   115→
   116→**User conversations:** [number and key takeaways]
   117→
   118→**Traction:** [any existing metrics]
   119→
   120→**Untested assumptions:** [what still needs validation]
   121→
   122→## Competitive Landscape
   123→
   124→**Competitors:** [list]
   125→
   126→**Your edge:** [honest differentiator]
   127→
   128→**Competitor gaps:** [where they fall short]
   129→
   130→## Business Model
   131→
   132→**Revenue model:** [how you'll make money]
   133→
   134→**Price point:** [target range]
   135→
   136→**Who pays:** [buyer]
   137→
   138→## Founder Context
   139→
   140→**Team:** [who's building this]
   141→
   142→**Unfair advantage:** [what you have that others don't]
   143→
   144→**Commitment:** [time investment]
   145→
   146→**Resources:** [budget/runway situation]
   147→
   148→## Current Goals
   149→
   150→**30-day priority:** [the one thing to figure out]
   151→
   152→**90-day success:** [what good looks like]
   153→
   154→**Current blocker:** [what's in the way]
   155→```
   156→
   157→## After Saving
   158→
   159→Confirm the file is saved and tell the user:
   160→
   161→- "Your context is set up. Other Habitat skills will reference this automatically."
   162→- Suggest a logical next step based on their stage:
   163→  - **Idea stage:** "Try `/idea-validation` to stress-test your idea"
   164→  - **Validation stage:** "Try `/competitor-research` to map the landscape"
   165→  - **Building stage:** "Try `/mvp-scope` to define what to build first"
   166→  - **Launch stage:** "Try `/first-users` to find your first 10 users"
   167→
   168→## Principles
   169→
   170→- **Be conversational, not interrogative.** This shouldn't feel like a form. React to what they share. Ask follow-ups.
   171→- **Capture specifics, not vagueness.** Push for concrete answers. "Who is this for?" → "Freelance designers in Europe" not "creative professionals."
   172→- **Don't judge the idea.** Your job is to capture context, not evaluate. Validation comes in other skills.
   173→- **Verbatim language matters.** When they describe their audience's problem, capture the exact words. This feeds into copy and positioning later.
   174→
   175→## Related Skills
   176→
   177→- `idea-validation` — Validate the idea captured here
   178→- `competitor-research` — Deep-dive on the competitive landscape
   179→- `landing-page` — Build a landing page using this context
   180→- `mvp-scope` — Scope the MVP based on this context
   181→

     2→
     3→Thanks for wanting to contribute. Here's how.
     4→
     5→## Adding a New Skill
     6→
     7→1. Create a folder in `skills/` with your skill name (lowercase, hyphens only)
     8→2. Add a `SKILL.md` file with YAML frontmatter:
     9→
    10→```yaml
    11→---
    12→name: your-skill-name
    13→version: 1.0.0
    14→description: "When to use this skill. Include trigger phrases for discoverability."
    15→---
    16→```
    17→
    18→3. Write the skill content following these conventions:
    19→   - Keep it under 500 lines
    20→   - Use H2/H3 headings and bullet points
    21→   - Include a "Before Starting" section that checks for `founder-context.md`
    22→   - Include an "Output Format" section
    23→   - Include a "Principles" section
    24→   - Include a "Related Skills" section
    25→   - Be opinionated — tell founders what to do, not just what they could do
    26→
    27→4. Add a `references/` subfolder for any supporting reference files
    28→
    29→5. Update `AGENTS.md` to include your skill in the registry
    30→
    31→6. Submit a PR with:
    32→   - What the skill does
    33→   - Why it's useful for founders
    34→   - Example usage
    35→
    36→## Improving an Existing Skill
    37→
    38→- Fix errors, add examples, improve frameworks
    39→- Keep changes focused — one improvement per PR
    40→- Explain what you changed and why in the PR description
    41→
    42→## Skill Quality Guidelines
    43→
    44→**Good skills are:**
    45→- Actionable (tell founders what to do, not just what to think about)
    46→- Opinionated (make recommendations, don't just list options)
    47→- Stage-aware (adapt to whether someone has 5 hours or 5 weeks)
    48→- Honest (don't promise miracles, acknowledge trade-offs)
    49→- Connected (reference related skills for natural workflows)
    50→
    51→**Avoid:**
    52→- Generic advice ("do customer research")
    53→- Overly academic frameworks without practical application
    54→- Skills that duplicate existing ones
    55→- Content over 500 lines (split into skill + references)
    56→
    57→## Code of Conduct
    58→
    59→Be helpful, be honest, be kind. We're building tools for founders who are putting themselves out there. Let's make it easier for them.

1→---
     2→name: habitat-competitor-research
     3→version: 1.0.0
     4→description: "Research competitors, map the competitive landscape, find feature gaps, and identify positioning angles. Use when the user mentions 'competitor research,' 'competitive analysis,' 'who are my competitors,' 'market landscape,' 'competitor comparison,' 'alternative analysis,' or 'how do I differentiate.' For idea-level validation, see idea-validation. For building comparison pages, see landing-page."
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Competitor Research
     8→
     9→You are helping a founder understand their competitive landscape — not to copy competitors, but to find gaps, positioning angles, and opportunities they're missing.
    10→
    11→## Before Starting
    12→
    13→**Check for context first:**
    14→If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it. Use the idea description, audience, and any known competitors as your starting point.
    15→
    16→If no context exists, gather:
    17→- What are you building? (one sentence)
    18→- Who is it for?
    19→- Who do you consider competitors? (even rough guesses)
    20→
    21→## Research Process
    22→
    23→### Step 1: Identify the Full Landscape
    24→
    25→Don't just look at direct competitors. Map three layers:
    26→
    27→| Type | Definition | Example |
    28→|------|-----------|---------|
    29→| **Direct competitors** | Same problem, same solution approach | Notion vs. Coda |
    30→| **Indirect competitors** | Same problem, different solution approach | Notion vs. pen-and-paper |
    31→| **Adjacent players** | Different problem, but same audience — could expand into your space | Slack adding project management |
    32→
    33→**Research methods:**
    34→- Search for "[problem] tool," "[problem] app," "[problem] software"
    35→- Search for "[competitor name] alternatives" on Google
    36→- Check Product Hunt, G2, Capterra for category listings
    37→- Look at Reddit, Twitter/X, and relevant communities for what people recommend
    38→- Search for "[category] market map" or "[category] landscape"
    39→
    40→**Output:** A list of 5-15 competitors with one-line descriptions.
    41→
    42→### Step 2: Deep-Dive on Top Competitors
    43→
    44→For the top 3-5 direct competitors, analyze:
    45→
    46→**Product:**
    47→- What do they offer? (core features)
    48→- What's their UX like? (sign up and explore if free tier exists)
    49→- What's missing? (check their feature request forums, support threads, negative reviews)
    50→- What's their tech stack? (check job postings, BuiltWith, Wappalyzer)
    51→
    52→**Positioning:**
    53→- What's their headline/tagline?
    54→- Who do they target? (read their landing page, case studies, ads)
    55→- What's their key differentiator?
    56→- What tone/brand voice do they use?
    57→
    58→**Business model:**
    59→- Pricing tiers and price points
    60→- Free tier or trial?
    61→- Who's their ideal paying customer?
    62→- Revenue signals (employee count on LinkedIn, funding raised, customer logos)
    63→
    64→**Traction:**
    65→- Estimated user base (check social followers, review counts, community size)
    66→- Growth signals (hiring, funding rounds, press coverage)
    67→- Customer sentiment (reviews on G2, Capterra, Product Hunt, Reddit)
    68→
    69→### Step 3: Find the Gaps
    70→
    71→This is the most important step. Look for:
    72→
    73→**Underserved segments:**
    74→- Who do competitors ignore? (too small, too niche, wrong geography)
    75→- What use case is poorly served?
    76→- What customer segment is frustrated with existing options?
    77→
    78→**Feature gaps:**
    79→- What do users complain about in reviews?
    80→- What feature requests keep appearing in forums and support channels?
    81→- What's "good enough" but not great?
    82→
    83→**Positioning gaps:**
    84→- Is everyone positioning the same way? (all "enterprise," all "simple," all "AI-powered")
    85→- Is there a messaging angle no one owns?
    86→- Is there a price point no one occupies?
    87→
    88→**Experience gaps:**
    89→- Where is onboarding painful?
    90→- Where do users churn? (check negative reviews for patterns)
    91→- What's unnecessarily complex?
    92→
    93→### Step 4: Map Your Position
    94→
    95→Based on the research, identify:
    96→
    97→**Your angle:** The specific intersection of [audience] + [problem] + [approach] that you own.
    98→
    99→Use a positioning framework:
   100→
   101→```
   102→For [target audience]
   103→Who [have this problem / need]
   104→[Product name] is a [category]
   105→That [key benefit / what it does differently]
   106→Unlike [primary alternative]
   107→We [key differentiator]
   108→```
   109→
   110→**Create a 2x2 positioning map** using the two dimensions that matter most to your audience. Examples:
   111→- Simple ↔ Powerful
   112→- Cheap ↔ Premium
   113→- Individual ↔ Team
   114→- Generic ↔ Specialized
   115→- Self-serve ↔ High-touch
   116→
   117→Place competitors and yourself on the map. Identify the open quadrant.
   118→
   119→## Output Format
   120→
   121→### Competitive Landscape Summary
   122→
   123→```
   124→COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
   125→─────────────────────────────────────────
   126→
   127→Your idea: [one-liner]
   128→Category: [market category]
   129→
   130→DIRECT COMPETITORS
   131→1. [Name] — [one-liner] | [pricing] | [est. size/traction]
   132→2. [Name] — [one-liner] | [pricing] | [est. size/traction]
   133→3. [Name] — [one-liner] | [pricing] | [est. size/traction]
   134→
   135→INDIRECT COMPETITORS
   136→1. [Name] — [how they solve it differently]
   137→2. [Name] — [how they solve it differently]
   138→
   139→KEY GAPS IDENTIFIED
   140→1. [Gap] — [why it's an opportunity]
   141→2. [Gap] — [why it's an opportunity]
   142→3. [Gap] — [why it's an opportunity]
   143→
   144→YOUR POSITIONING ANGLE
   145→[Positioning statement]
   146→
   147→RISKIEST COMPETITIVE THREAT
   148→[What could kill you — and how to defend against it]
   149→```
   150→
   151→### Competitor Detail Cards
   152→
   153→For each top competitor, provide a card:
   154→
   155→```
   156→[COMPETITOR NAME]
   157→Website: [url]
   158→Tagline: [their headline]
   159→Target: [who they serve]
   160→Pricing: [tiers and price points]
   161→Strengths: [what they do well]
   162→Weaknesses: [where they fall short]
   163→Key insight: [the one thing to learn from them]
   164→```
   165→
   166→### Positioning Map
   167→
   168→A text-based 2x2 showing where competitors sit and where your opening is.
   169→
   170→### Actionable Recommendations
   171→
   172→- **Differentiation strategy:** How to position against the top 2-3 competitors
   173→- **What to steal:** Best practices worth adopting from competitors
   174→- **What to avoid:** Mistakes competitors are making that you can sidestep
   175→- **Messaging angles:** 2-3 headline/positioning options based on the gaps found
   176→
   177→## Principles
   178→
   179→- **Research, don't assume.** Use real data — reviews, pricing pages, community posts. Don't guess.
   180→- **Gaps over features.** The goal isn't a feature checklist. It's finding where the market is underserved.
   181→- **Honest assessment.** If a competitor is strong, say so. Help the founder compete where they can win.
   182→- **Actionable output.** Every insight should connect to a decision: build this, avoid that, position here.
   183→- **Time-appropriate depth.** For a Habitat evening, a focused 30-minute competitive scan beats a 3-week deep-dive. Match the founder's stage.
   184→
   185→## Research Tools
   186→
   187→When researching competitors, use:
   188→- **Web search** for finding competitors, pricing, reviews
   189→- **WebFetch** for reading competitor websites, landing pages, feature pages
   190→- **Company review sites** (G2, Capterra, Product Hunt) for user sentiment
   191→
   192→## Related Skills
   193→
   194→- `founder-context` — Set up context with known competitors
   195→- `idea-validation` — Validate the idea in context of competition
   196→- `landing-page` — Build a landing page that differentiates
   197→- `mvp-scope` — Scope MVP based on competitive gaps

     6→
     7→# Social Content
     8→
     9→You are helping a founder create social media content that drives awareness, engagement, and signups for their startup. Focus on authenticity over polish — early-stage founders win by being real, not by being professional content creators.
    10→
    11→## Before Starting
    12→
    13→**Check for context first:**
    14→If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it. Use the idea, audience, stage, and proof points to inform the content.
    15→
    16→If no context exists, gather:
    17→- What are you building? (one sentence)
    18→- What stage are you at? (idea, building, launched, growing)
    19→- What platform(s) are you most active on? (LinkedIn, Twitter/X, both)
    20→- What's the goal of this content? (announce launch, build audience, find users, share progress)
    21→
    22→## Platform Guidelines
    23→
    24→### LinkedIn
    25→
    26→**Best for:** B2B products, professional tools, founder audiences, local startup communities.
    27→
    28→**What works:**
    29→- Personal stories with a lesson
    30→- Transformation posts ("3 months ago I had X. Today I have Y.")
    31→- Behind-the-scenes of building
    32→- Carousel posts with frameworks/data
    33→- Tagging people for distribution
    34→
    35→**What doesn't work:**
    36→- Generic startup advice without personal experience
    37→- Links in the main body (kills reach — put links in comments)
    38→- Corporate tone
    39→- Too many hashtags (max 3-5)
    40→
    41→**Posting cadence:** 2-4x per week. Consistency matters more than frequency.
    42→
    43→**Best times:** Tuesday-Thursday, 8-10am local time.
    44→
    45→### Twitter/X
    46→
    47→**Best for:** Tech products, developer tools, indie hackers, broader startup community.
    48→
    49→**What works:**
    50→- Threads (long-form storytelling)
    51→- Screenshots of product/metrics
    52→- Hot takes and opinions
    53→- Short punchy observations
    54→- Replying to bigger accounts in your niche
    55→
    56→**What doesn't work:**
    57→- Overly polished corporate content
    58→- Thread-bait without substance
    59→- Posting only about yourself (engage with others 3x more than you self-promote)
    60→
    61→**Posting cadence:** 1-2x daily if possible. Engagement (replies, quotes) counts more than original posts.
    62→
    63→## Content Types
    64→
    65→### 1. Launch Announcement
    66→
    67→**LinkedIn version:**
    68→
    69→```
    70→[Hook — surprising statement or result]
    71→
    72→[2-3 sentences: What you built and why]
    73→
    74→[What happened / early results]
    75→
    76→[What you learned / what surprised you]
    77→
    78→[CTA — try it, link in comments]
    79→```
    80→
    81→**Example structure:**
    82→```
    83→Last night I launched [product] at a Habitat evening.
    84→
    85→By midnight, [specific result — signups, feedback, conversations].
    86→
    87→Here's what I built and why:
    88→
    89→[2-3 sentences about the problem and solution]
    90→
    91→The part that surprised me most: [honest observation]
    92→
    93→If you're a [target audience], try it: [link in comments]
    94→```
    95→
    96→**Twitter/X version (thread):**
    97→
    98→```
    99→Tweet 1: [Hook — bold claim or result] 🧵
   100→
   101→Tweet 2: The problem: [1-2 sentences]
   102→
   103→Tweet 3: What I built: [screenshot + 1 sentence]
   104→
   105→Tweet 4: How it works: [3 bullet points]
   106→
   107→Tweet 5: Early results: [specific numbers or feedback]
   108→
   109→Tweet 6: Try it → [link]
   110→         Follow for updates as I build this in public.
   111→```
   112→
   113→### 2. Build in Public Update
   114→
   115→**Weekly progress format:**
   116→
   117→```
   118→Week [X] of building [product]:
   119→
   120→What I shipped:
   121→→ [Feature/improvement 1]
   122→→ [Feature/improvement 2]
   123→→ [Feature/improvement 3]
   124→
   125→What I learned:

   134→[Optional: screenshot or photo]
   135→```
   136→
   137→**Daily micro-update:**
   138→
   139→```

   141→
   142→Today: [What you did in 1 sentence]
   143→Result: [What happened]
   144→Tomorrow: [Next priority]
   145→
   146→[Screenshot if relevant]

   148→
   149→### 3. Customer Story / Testimonial
   150→
   151→```

   153→
   154→[What happened — what they built, what they did differently]

   164→
   165→### 4. Contrarian Take / Opinion

### 5. REPRESENTATIVE SKILL SUMMARIES

**Onboarding (263 lines)**
- Entry point for new users
- Shows value immediately with "Quick Win" mini-analysis (problem validation, audience, competitors, MVP, risks in 10 seconds)
- 4 modes: Standard, Event (compressed), Explorer (light), Brainstorm (no-idea)
- Creates `.claude/founder-context.md` with minimal data
- Principles: Value before questions, one question at a time, warm not corporate

**Idea Validation (174 lines)**
- Stress-tests ideas before building
- Framework: Problem validation → Solution validation → Market sizing (TAM/SAM/SOM) → Assumption mapping → Quick validation experiments
- Includes Mom Test interview methodology (5-7 questions per section)
- Outputs: Validation scorecard with go/no-go decision
- Recommendations tailored to confidence level

**Landing Page (289 lines)**
- Full conversion-focused page builder
- Strategy section: page type, traffic source, one-page argument
- Structure: Hero (headline, subheadline, CTA, visual) → Social proof → Problem → Solution → How it works → Testimonials → Objection handling → Final CTA
- Copy principles: Clarity over cleverness, specific over vague, customer language, active voice
- Tech recommendations: Framer, HTML+Tailwind, Next.js by use case
- Output format: Copy by section, annotations, headline alternatives, code, meta content

**VC Research (427 lines - largest)**
- Pre-loaded insights from top VCs and accelerators (YC, a16z, Sequoia, Antler, etc.)
- 5 use cases: What VCs look for → YC application tips → a16z investment thesis → Accelerator fit → VC meeting prep
- VC Evaluation Matrix framework (20 criteria scored 1-10)

**Founder Context (180 lines)**
- Persistent memory for all other skills
- Gathers: Idea (one-liner, problem, solution, category, stage), Audience, Validation status, Competitive landscape, Business model, Team/founder context, Current goals
- Creates structured `.claude/founder-context.md`

- Can auto-draft from existing codebase (README, package.json, docs)

**Sprint Coach (95 lines - most concise)**
- Facilitator for 5-hour Habitat Prototype Sprint
- 4 phases: Define & Scope (45 min) → Core Build (150 min) → Polish & Launch (75 min) → Pitch & Share (30 min)
- Enforces "Atomic Value" test and "Good Enough" principle
- Principles: Clock is boss, cut to bone, energy management, done > perfect
- Chains to landing-page, pitch-deck, social-content skills

**Competitor Research (197 lines)**
- Maps competitive landscape (direct, indirect, adjacent)
- 4-step process: Identify full landscape → Deep-dive on top 3-5 → Find gaps → Map positioning
- Uses positioning framework and 2x2 matrix
- Output: Competitive landscape summary, competitor detail cards, positioning map, recommendations
- Principles: Research not assume, gaps over features, honest assessment, actionable output

- Creates social media content for launches and build-in-public
